authoritative_sources.title: IATA Ground Operations Manual
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IATA ground operations manuals are recognized airline and ground-handler operating references for aircraft turnaround, load-control interfaces, ramp safety, aircraft servicing, GSE, and ground handling terminology.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: turnaround readiness, ramp-service execution, GSE dependency review, service-log completion, and exception handling
used_for: GSE and aircraft servicing dependency handling

authoritative_sources.title: IATA Airport Handling Manual
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The IATA Airport Handling Manual and Standard Ground Handling Agreement framework are recognized industry references for airline-handler service scope, operational handoffs, service-level expectations, and boundary language.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: handler scope classification, SGHA or SLA dependency review, service handoff, and completion evidence control
used_for: tenant adaptation and conflicting-policy scenario design

authoritative_sources.title: Manual on Ground Handling (Doc 10121)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ICAO is the United Nations specialized aviation agency, and Doc 10121 is an official ground handling manual for safe and efficient ground operations and oversight expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ground handling safety baseline, operational oversight, exception routing, and ramp-service risk boundaries

authoritative_sources.title: 14 CFR 139.329 - Pedestrians and ground vehicles
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This airport certification rule directly governs pedestrian and ground vehicle controls at certificated airports, making it authoritative for ramp vehicle, movement-area, and safety-boundary escalation.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ramp vehicle and pedestrian prerequisite review, GSE movement blocker handling, and safety escalation
used_for: GSE and towing safety refusal triggers

authoritative_sources.title: AC 150/5210-20A - Ground Vehicle Operations to include Taxiing or Towing an Aircraft on Airports
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FAA airport safety guidance addresses ground vehicle, pedestrian, taxiing, towing, movement area, non-movement area, apron, and runway safety area controls relevant to GSE movement and aircraft towing escalation.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: towing, GSE route, ramp movement dependency review, tow-path disruption handling, and safety-owner routing
used_for: tow, apron, movement-area, and vehicle-control terminology
route or schedule disruption with compliance impact

authoritative_sources.title: IATA Baggage Tracking - Resolution 753
authoritative_sources.source_class: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IATA Resolution 753 defines baggage tracking points and audit trail expectations for acceptance, loading, transfer, and arrival that airlines, airports, and handlers use for baggage custody visibility.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: baggage custody review, transfer exception handling, handoff evidence capture, and passenger-impact escalation
used_for: handoff evidence and missing scan failure mode

authoritative_sources.title: AMS Air Features
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CBP is the U.S. customs authority, and its Air AMS guidance explains advance electronic air cargo information expectations that constrain customs-dependent cargo movement and release boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: air cargo documentation gap review, customs-owner routing, and cargo movement blocker escalation
used_for: air waybill, house shipment, and AMS evidence handling

authoritative_sources.title: 19 CFR 122.48a - Electronic information for air cargo required in advance of arrival
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This customs regulation defines advance electronic air cargo information requirements for inbound air cargo and supports escalation when ground handling is asked to clear or release customs-dependent cargo movement.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: air cargo documentation prerequisite validation, customs blocker handling, and adjacent customs/compliance routing

authoritative_sources.title: 49 CFR Part 1542 - Airport Security
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Part 1542 governs U.S. airport operator security programs, access controls, and security areas that ground handling must respect and route rather than decide.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: security-sensitive exception handling, AOA or restricted-area boundary review, and adjacent-owner routing
used_for: refusal triggers for access or security determinations

workflow_stages.name: Accepted intake and authority classification
workflow_stages.description: Normalize the requested ground handling outcome, affected flight, tail, stand, gate, ramp bay, route, baggage, cargo, ULD, GSE, service, custody, equipment, documentation, and tenant service-scope records, then classify the work as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked before execution progresses.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Prerequisite and system-of-record validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate turn plans, station messages, ramp logs, load instructions, baggage scans, BRS or BHS records, cargo manifests, air waybill and house shipment data, ULD records, service logs, GSE readiness, maintenance status, customs documentation, security boundaries, SGHA or SLA scope, and tenant SOP context.
source_classes: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Lifecycle progression and turnaround execution
workflow_stages.description: Move accepted work through accepted, in-progress, evidence-reviewed, blocked, rework-pending, escalated, completed, or returned states only when required records, owners, service evidence, custody evidence, and dependency status support the transition.
source_classes: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Exception queue and irregular-operations handling
workflow_stages.description: Capture GSE faults, staffing or equipment shortages, stand changes, tow restrictions, ramp congestion, missing load instructions, baggage scan breaks, cargo documentation conflicts, service-log gaps, passenger impact, and safety signals as exception summaries with next owners and rework criteria.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Adjacent authority routing
workflow_stages.description: Route dispatch release, ATC or airport movement clearance, load-control release, customs release, TSA or airport security, dangerous goods acceptance, maintenance return-to-service, emergency response, customer recovery, warehouse, and safety-critical decisions to the correct owner instead of resolving them inside ground handling.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance

workflow_stages.name: Completion evidence and audit-ready closure
workflow_stages.description: Confirm closure only when lifecycle state, source-system evidence, service-log completion, handoff or destination-owner acknowledgement, blocker resolution, and residual risk posture are documented; otherwise refuse completion and keep the state reversible.
source_classes: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

artifacts: turn plans, station control records, ramp logs, airline station messages, and irregular-operations tickets
flight, tail, gate, stand, ramp bay, belt, route, tow path, STA, ETA, STD, ETD, ATD, TOBT, TSAT, and service-level records
load instruction, load plan, load sheet status, NOTOC references where applicable, ULD or bulk load plans, and load-control owner records
baggage acceptance, make-up, offload, transfer, reconciliation, BRS, BHS, missing-bag, and chain-of-custody handoff records
cargo booking, air waybill, house shipment, cargo manifest, warehouse, ULD, mail, transfer, customs, security screening, embargo, and custody records
GSE roster and status for tugs, belt loaders, high loaders, dollies, GPUs, PCA, stairs, pushback tractors, deicing equipment, lavatory, water, and service vehicles

maintenance work orders, equipment defect records, return-to-service references, airport ramp rules, FOD and safety observations, incident notes, service logs, SGHA or SLA excerpts, tenant SOPs, security program constraints, and owner maps
systems_of_record: station control or turn management system
ramp operations log and handler work-order queue
FIDS, AODB, gate management, stand management, and resource management systems
BHS, BRS, baggage reconciliation, and baggage tracing systems
cargo booking, warehouse, manifest, ULD, and customs documentation systems
GSE fleet, telematics, equipment-status, and service-log systems
security access-control and airport security program systems
customer operations, CRM, and service-desk systems
tenant station SOP, SGHA, SLA, airport rules, and owner-map repositories

allowed: Classify ground handling requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Advance, hold, rework, escalate, return, or complete handler-owned work based on documented lifecycle state and evidence.

Produce execution status updates, exception summaries, movement or turnaround summaries, service-log summaries, chain-of-custody notes, irregular-operations escalation notes, documentation gap notes, and completion evidence records.
Route work to dispatch, load control, customs, security, maintenance, warehouse, airport operations, customer operations, aviation compliance, or human reviewers when ownership shifts.
escalate: Turn plan, load instruction, baggage, cargo, ULD, GSE, service, maintenance, customs, security, custody, service-level, passenger-impact, or handoff evidence is missing or materially conflicts.

Movement depends on dispatch release, ATC clearance, airport movement approval, final load-control release, customs release, TSA or airport security determination, dangerous goods acceptance, maintenance return-to-service, emergency response, or safety-critical approval.
Public baseline sources, airport rules, tenant SOPs, SGHA or SLA terms, airline procedures, customs or security requirements, labor rules, or jurisdiction-specific rules materially conflict.

Owner mapping is ambiguous across ground handling, airline station control, airport operations, dispatch, load control, warehouse, maintenance, security, customs, cargo, baggage, or customer operations teams.
refuse: Issue or imply flight dispatch release, ATC clearance, airport movement clearance, final load-control approval, customs release, TSA security determination, dangerous goods acceptance approval, maintenance return-to-service approval, legal interpretation, financial approval, emergency command, or safety signoff.
Mark work complete without required source-system evidence, service-log evidence, custody or handoff acknowledgement, and blocker resolution.
Invent custody proof, cargo documentation status, baggage scan state, service completion, equipment status, route status, load status, or completion evidence not present in authoritative records.
Bypass safety, security, customs, maintenance, load-control, dispatch, service, custody, or condition controls to preserve a schedule or service-level target.

domain_failure_modes: turnaround work is marked complete without service logs, baggage reconciliation, cargo handoff, GSE status, or destination-owner evidence
baggage, cargo, mail, or ULD custody breaks are hidden by generic completion notes instead of missing-scan or handoff exception records
customs, security, or dangerous goods documentation gaps are treated as routine movement blockers that ground handling can clear
GSE faults, tow-path restrictions, ramp congestion, FOD, or restricted-area movement issues are advanced without safety or airport movement owner review
load-control or dispatch authority is implied by ground handling because a station owner wants fast closure
airline service-level pressure causes the lane to promise departure, pushback, loading, customs, or safety outcomes outside its authority
systems of record disagree on flight, stand, belt, equipment, baggage, cargo, ULD, load, service, customs, or completion status and the conflict is not escalated
tenant-local shortcuts override IATA, ICAO, FAA, TSA, CBP, airport rule, SGHA, SLA, or station SOP evidence requirements

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Route or schedule disruption with compliance impact
Request that implies customs, dispatch, or safety authority

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Station SOPs, SGHA scope, airline SLAs, airport ramp rules, security program details, customs procedures, dangerous goods handling procedures, load-control process, labor rules, and owner maps vary materially by tenant, airport, carrier, and handler.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: This pack uses IATA, ICAO, FAA, eCFR, TSA, and CBP sources as the public baseline. Runtime must retrieve tenant facility, carrier, handler, jurisdiction, service model, system-of-record, owner map, station SOP, SGHA or SLA, airport rules, security, customs, dangerous goods, and load-control context before applying tenant-specific outcomes.

notes: The research baseline covers workflow, evidence, terminology, movement and custody handling, safety, customs and security boundaries, equipment and route implications, service-level handling, and failure behavior rather than only generic airport labels.

The lane stops at ground handling execution support. Dispatch release, final load-control release, customs release, TSA or airport security determinations, ATC or airport movement clearance, maintenance return-to-service, dangerous goods acceptance, legal interpretation, emergency command, financial approval, and safety signoff remain outside the lane even when operational pressure is high.
